title: Chapter 2 Quiz
description: Test your mastery over Rust's strict variables, mutability, shadowing, and scope!

questions.question: By default, variables declared with `let` in Rust are...
questions.explanation: Variables are immutable by default in Rust, meaning their value cannot be changed once bound.

questions.question: Which keyword is used to make a variable's value changeable?
questions.explanation: Placing `mut` after `let` makes the variable mutable so its value can be changed.

questions.question: You can change the data type of a variable declared with `let mut` without using shadowing.
questions.explanation: `mut` only allows changing the *value*, not the *type*. To change the type, you must use shadowing.

questions.question: If you try to reassign an immutable variable, the Rust _____ will throw an error and refuse to build your code.
questions.explanation: Rust catches mutability errors entirely at compile-time.

questions.question: How do you correctly declare a constant in Rust?
questions.explanation: Constants must be declared with `const`, their names typically in uppercase, and their data type MUST be explicitly written.

questions.question: A constant can only be set to a value computed at runtime (like the result of reading a file).
questions.explanation: Constants must be evaluated entirely at compile-time. They cannot depend on runtime operations.

questions.question: What happens if you type `const mut SCORE: u32 = 0;`?
options: It creates a global mutable state
It throws a syntax error because constants can never be mutable
questions.explanation: Constants are permanently immutable. You cannot ever combine `const` with `mut`.

questions.question: Redeclaring a new variable with the exact same name as an existing variable is known as variable _____.
questions.explanation: Shadowing occurs when a new `let` declaration uses the same name, hiding the original variable.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between using `mut` and using Shadowing?
options: `mut` allows changing the type, Shadowing does not
Shadowing allows changing the type via a new `let` statement, whereas `mut` only changes the value
questions.explanation: Because shadowing technically creates a brand new variable, it is allowed to hold a completely different data type than the original.

questions.question: When you shadow a variable, the new variable created is automatically mutable.
questions.explanation: No, the newly shadowed variable is immutable by default unless you declare it with `let mut`.

questions.question: Which of these characters represents the start of a Scope in Rust?
questions.explanation: Curly braces `{ }` define a block of code, which creates a new scope.

questions.question: A variable declared inside an inner block (nested scope) can be accessed by the outer scope after the block ends.
questions.explanation: Variables die and their memory is freed immediately when their scope (the closing `}`) ends.

questions.question: If you shadow an outer variable specifically inside an inner scope `{ }`, what happens when the inner scope ends?
options: The outer variable remains permanently changed
The inner shadow is destroyed, and the outer variable is revealed in its original state
questions.explanation: Shadowing inside an inner scope only lasts as long as that scope. Once it ends, the original outer variable comes back.

questions.question: Rust naming conventions prefer `MAX_CARS` for constants, but `_____` (lowercase with underscores) for normal variables.
questions.explanation: Rust standardizes on `snake_case` for variable and function names.

questions.question: You can shadow a `mut` variable into an immutable variable.
questions.explanation: Yes, you can redeclare a `let mut x = ...` as `let x = ...`. Because it's a completely new variable, you can enforce immutability going forward.
This is shadowing in action! We declare a new `a` over and over again without needing `mut`. The result is (5+1)*2 = 12.

questions.question: Why does Rust enforce immutability by default?
options: To guarantee memory safety and prevent unexpected bugs in complex concurrent programs
Just to be different from C++
questions.explanation: Immutable data cannot be changed maliciously or accidentally by other parts of the code passing through multiple threads.

questions.question: The time it takes for a Rust program to be translated into executable binary code is called _____-time.
questions.explanation: Compile-time is when the Rust compiler is checking all your mutability and scopes.

questions.question: Constants are allowed to be declared in the global scope (outside of `fn main`).
questions.explanation: Unlike standard variables which usually live inside blocks or functions, `const` declarations can be placed globally for the entire file to access.

options: Yes, it shadows the string with an integer, then makes the new integer mutable.
No, because you can't use mut and shadowing together.
No, because the types don't match.
questions.explanation: Yes! The second `let` statement shadows the original, creates a brand new variable that holds an integer, and marks the *new* variable as mutable.
